reasoning:
The reference value for one plus the 30-day average of protests in Syria over the past 360 days is approximately 95.167. Ten times this reference value is ~952. For the question to resolve positively, ACLED would need to record more than 952 'Protests' in a single 30-day period leading up to the resolution date (an average of >31.7 protests per day). 

Searches regarding ACLED data for Syria reveal that recent massive waves of demonstrations or critical events (such as the 30 protest events across the southern region in March 2025 regarding Israel, or 80+ protests over a whole month against HTS in 2024) are considered notable and routinely highlighted by ACLED. Achieving nearly 1,000 recorded discrete in-person, non-violent demonstrations in a single month requires an incredibly dense, sustained mobilization across dozens of locations simultaneously every single day over a 30-day period. Syria currently does not exhibit any news indicating such a historically unprecedented, country-wide peaceful protest wave. While the country is settling politically following the fall of Assad, protests are relatively localized. 

Furthermore, the first resolution date (2025-06-15) is only 7 days away, meaning the 30-day period has almost entirely elapsed, and there is no trace of an ongoing 950+ protest mobilization. As such, the likelihood of clearing this exceptionally high standard is near the absolute structural floor. Because uncertainty increases with time, the probability gently increases but stays strictly near the bottom margin.
